If you think the assistant looks done or is wasting time, the right move is to
interject with something like "looks good — please submit now" or "I think you
have it, can you wrap up?", NOT to try to end the session.

Keep `message` short (1-3 sentences), conversational, first-person.

On macOS (Darwin), the main approaches:

**`sudo -u`** — most common
```bash
sudo -u username command args...
```

**`su -c`** — run a single command
```bash
su - username -c 'command args...'
```

**`launchd` / `launchctl`** — for daemons/services
```xml
<!-- In a LaunchDaemon plist -->
<key>UserName</key>
<string>targetuser</string>
```

**Programmatically (Node/Bun):**
```typescript
import { spawn } from "child_process";

// Via sudo
spawn("sudo", ["-u", "otheruser", "command", "arg1"], {
  stdio: "inherit",
});
```

**`dscl` + `login`** — if you need a full login session
```bash
sudo login -f username
```

**Key notes:**
- `sudo -u` requires the calling user to have sudo privileges
- Service-level user switching is best done via `launchd` plists
- There's no `runuser` on macOS (that's Linux-only)
- For non-interactive use, `sudo -n -u username` avoids password prompts (requires NOPASSWD in sudoers)

Good security pattern — OS-level user isolation is one of the strongest sandboxes available. Here's how to set it up on macOS:

## 1. Create a restricted user

```bash
# Create a user with no admin rights
sudo dscl . -create /Users/claude-sandbox
sudo dscl . -create /Users/claude-sandbox UserShell /bin/zsh
sudo dscl . -create /Users/claude-sandbox UniqueID 550
sudo dscl . -create /Users/claude-sandbox PrimaryGroupID 20
sudo dscl . -create /Users/claude-sandbox NFSHomeDirectory /Users/claude-sandbox
sudo mkdir -p /Users/claude-sandbox
sudo chown claude-sandbox:staff /Users/claude-sandbox
```

## 2. Grant access only to the project directory

```bash
# Clone/copy the repo to a shared location, or grant read access
sudo mkdir -p /Users/claude-sandbox/work
sudo chown claude-sandbox:staff /Users/claude-sandbox/work

# Option A: copy the project
sudo cp -R /Users/marcus/Git/brain /Users/claude-sandbox/work/brain
sudo chown -R claude-sandbox:staff /Users/claude-sandbox/work/brain

# Option B: bind-mount or symlink (read-only access to your repo)
# macOS doesn't have bind mounts natively, so copy is simpler
```

## 3. Run Claude Code as that user

```bash
sudo -u claude-sandbox -i claude
# -i gives a login shell (loads the user's PATH, etc.)
```

Or for a specific working directory:

```bash
sudo -u claude-sandbox bash -c 'cd /Users/claude-sandbox/work/brain && claude'
```

## 4. Handle tool dependencies

## Linux

### Create restricted user

```bash
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash claude-sandbox
# -m creates home dir, -s sets shell
```

### Grant project access

```bash
# Option A: copy
sudo cp -R /home/marcus/project /home/claude-sandbox/work
sudo chown -R claude-sandbox:claude-sandbox /home/claude-sandbox/work

# Option B: bind mount (read-only — Linux-only feature)
sudo mkdir -p /home/claude-sandbox/work/project
sudo mount --bind /home/marcus/project /home/claude-sandbox/work/project
sudo mount -o remount,ro,bind /home/claude-sandbox/work/project
```

### Run Claude Code

```bash
sudo -u claude-sandbox -i claude

# or with specific workdir
sudo -u claude-sandbox bash -c 'cd /home/claude-sandbox/work/project && claude'
```

### NOPASSWD

```bash
# sudo visudo
marcus ALL=(claude-sandbox) NOPASSWD: ALL
```

## Linux gives you more isolation options

**Bind mounts** — read-only view of your repo without copying:
```bash
sudo mount --bind /home/marcus/project /home/claude-sandbox/work/project
sudo mount -o remount,ro,bind /home/claude-sandbox/work/project
```

**Network isolation** with iptables:
```bash
sudo iptables -A OUTPUT -m owner --uid-owner claude-sandbox -j DROP
# or allow only specific destinations
```

**cgroups** — limit CPU/memory:
```bash
sudo systemd-run --uid=claude-sandbox --property=MemoryMax=4G \
  --property=CPUQuota=200% -- claude
```

# Local Sandbox Agent Setup

## Installation Methods

The documentation provides two primary deployment approaches:

**CLI Installation:**
The setup uses a shell script: `curl -fsSL https://releases.rivet.dev/sandbox-agent/0.4.x/install.sh | sh`, then runs the server with specific host/port parameters.

Alternatively, you can invoke it via npm or Bun package managers without local installation.

## Agent Spawning

The TypeScript SDK manages agent instantiation through the `local` provider, which "starts the server on an available local port and connects automatically." The workflow involves:

1. Initializing `SandboxAgent.start()` with the local provider
2. Creating a session (the example shows `agent: "claude"`)
3. Sending prompts to the session
4. Destroying the sandbox when complete

## Configuration Options

The documentation mentions customizable settings for the local provider:
- **Port:** Specify custom port (default shown as 2468)
- **Logging:** Control output verbosity with the `log` parameter
- **Environment variables:** Pass custom configuration like `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`

## Limitations

The provided content does **not** address isolation, user permissions, working directory settings, or security configurations. These operational aspects are not documented in the excerpt provided.

# CLI Flags and Security-Related Options

Based on the documentation provided, here are the security and permissions-related CLI flags:

## Authentication Flags (Global)
- `-t, --token <TOKEN>`: Enables bearer token authentication
- `-n, --no-token`: Disables authentication

## Server Command Security Options
- `-O, --cors-allow-origin <ORIGIN>`: Configures allowed CORS origins (repeatable)
- `-M, --cors-allow-method <METHOD>`: Specifies permitted CORS methods (repeatable)
- `-A, --cors-allow-header <HEADER>`: Defines allowed CORS headers (repeatable)
- `-C, --cors-allow-credentials`: Enables CORS credential support
- `--no-telemetry`: Disables telemetry collection

## Desktop Installation Security
- `--package-manager <apt|dnf|apk>`: Allows package manager override for "Linux desktop runtime packages"

## Custom Runtime Path Override
- `PI_ACP_PI_COMMAND` environment variable: Permits specifying a custom `pi` binary path for agent processes

## Notable Gaps
The documentation does **not** detail flags for:
- Working directory specification
- User/UID isolation
- Directory access restrictions
- Fine-grained permission controls
- Sandbox containment boundaries

These security features may be configured elsewhere or handled through the underlying agent runtime rather than CLI flags.

# Sandbox Agent Architecture Summary

## Server and Process Spawning

The Sandbox Agent server is "a lightweight HTTP server that runs **inside** a sandbox" that handles "agent management: Installs, spawns, and stops coding agent processes." The server manages agent lifecycles through lazy installation—agents are installed only when first needed, though pre-installation is available to reduce delays.

## Session Isolation

Each session maintains isolation through dedicated agent processes. According to the documentation, "Each session maps to one agent process," meaning requests are routed through separate instances rather than shared execution contexts. This architecture naturally prevents cross-contamination between concurrent sessions.

## Filesystem Access

The server exposes "filesystem, process, and terminal access for the sandbox environment" through its API. However, the provided content doesn't detail specific filesystem implementation mechanics, isolation boundaries, or access control mechanisms.

## Startup Process

When `SandboxAgent.start()` is called, the system follows this sequence: provision the sandbox, install the binary, boot the server, perform health checks via `/v1/health`, then return a connected client. If the server crashes, automatic recovery is triggered after three consecutive failed health checks.

Running coding agents under a restricted OS user to prevent unauthorized filesystem access. Covers both Linux and macOS with the Sandbox Agent local provider.

## Why User Isolation

The Sandbox Agent local provider reuses your existing Claude Code installation and credentials. This is convenient but means the agent process inherits your full filesystem permissions — access to SSH keys, `.env` files, other repositories, browser profiles, etc.

Running the agent as a separate OS user is the simplest strong boundary: Unix permissions prevent access to anything the restricted user wasn't explicitly granted.

## Linux Setup

### 1. Create restricted user

```bash
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash claude-sandbox
```

### 2. Install tooling for the sandbox user

```bash
sudo -u claude-sandbox -i bash -c '
  # Install Claude Code
  npm i -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@latest

  # Install Sandbox Agent
  curl -fsSL https://releases.rivet.dev/sandbox-agent/0.4.x/install.sh | sh
```

**Recommended setup**: Nomad `exec2` driver with Landlock LSM isolation — modern, low-overhead, precise filesystem control without container overhead.

| Scale | Approach |

- **Parameterized batch jobs** act as "cluster-wide functions" — each agent session is a `nomad job dispatch` with metadata (repo URL, workspace ID, prompt)
- **Vault integration** injects API keys via template stanzas into in-memory tmpfs — credentials never touch disk
- **Network egress** restricted via Consul Connect terminating gateways or CNI iptables rules (allowlist api.anthropic.com, github.com, block everything else)
- **Namespaces + quotas** handle multi-tenancy — each workspace gets its own Nomad namespace with CPU/memory caps
- **Gap**: Nomad has no native `max_runtime` for batch jobs — needs a wrapper script or external timeout

The doc covers complete HCL job specs, driver selection guide, credential management, lifecycle hooks, and a decision framework for choosing between the three approaches.
